# Topic 2 - Convex Analysis

## Introduction

You now have three measuring instruments — three geometries for assessing the size of a vector or a function. What you do not yet have is a theory of when optimization problems are well-behaved. When does a minimum exist? When is it unique? When can you find it efficiently? When can you trust that a local minimum is the global minimum?

The answer to all four questions is the same single word: convexity.

Convexity is the structural property that separates tractable optimization from intractable optimization more cleanly than any other concept in mathematics. A convex optimization problem — one where the objective is a convex function and the feasible set is a convex set — has no local minima that are not global minima. It has efficient algorithms that find the global minimum exactly or to arbitrary precision. It has a rich duality theory — the Legendre-Fenchel transform of the next topic — that provides alternative formulations and certificates of optimality. And it has a single foundational inequality — Jensen's inequality — that propagates through information theory, probability, and the training objectives of every generative model in Phase 3.

You have encountered convexity before, though perhaps not by name. In MATH 201, when you found the minimum of a function by setting the derivative to zero and checking the second derivative was positive, you were implicitly using convexity — the second derivative test is a local convexity condition. In MATH 208, when you used Jensen's inequality to derive bounds on expectations, you were using the foundational inequality of this topic. What you are building now is the full geometric and analytic theory that makes those computations principled rather than procedural.

This topic has three tightly connected subtopics that build on each other in strict logical order.

The first subtopic is **convex sets** — the geometric objects over which convex optimization takes place. A convex set is one where the straight line between any two points stays inside the set. This simple geometric condition has profound consequences: it is what makes the feasible region of a convex optimization problem have no hidden pockets or disconnected components that could trap an optimizer. Every norm ball you studied in the previous topic is a convex set — the diamond, the sphere, and the hypercube are all convex — and convexity is precisely the condition that makes them valid constraint sets for well-behaved optimization.

The second subtopic is **convex functions** — the objectives that convex optimization minimizes. A convex function is one that lies below every chord connecting two points on its graph, or equivalently one whose second derivative is non-negative in every direction. Convex functions have the critical property that every local minimum is a global minimum — there is no valley that is lower than every nearby point but not the lowest point overall. This property is what makes gradient descent reliable: on a convex function, you can follow the gradient downhill and be guaranteed to reach the global minimum, not merely a local one.

The third subtopic is **Jensen's inequality** — the master inequality that emerges from convexity and propagates through all of probability theory. It states that for a convex function $\phi$ and a random variable $X$, the function of the expectation is always at most the expectation of the function: $\phi(\mathbb{E}[X]) \leq \mathbb{E}[\phi(X)]$. This single inequality is the foundation of the AM-GM inequality, the log-sum inequality, the data processing inequality, the evidence lower bound in variational inference, and the Kantorovich duality in Optimal Transport. It appears so frequently in the roadmap that you will eventually stop noticing it — it will simply be the air that probability theory breathes.

---
---
